name: Pyston and Pyjion
summary: Two minor Python JIT efforts grouped for completeness. Pyston (Dropbox 2014–2017, revived 2020+) JIT-compiles CPython with attribute-cache acceleration; Pyston-lite (2022) repackaged it as a CPython extension. Pyjion (Microsoft 2016–2021) used the .NET CLR's RyuJIT as codegen backend. Both mostly dormant; eclipsed by mainline JIT and CinderX.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Cinder is the dominant alternative Python JIT today. Two earlier efforts deserve historical mention. **Pyston** (originally Dropbox 2014–2017, revived 2020+) is a JIT-compiling CPython fork with attribute-cache acceleration, JIT-compiled bytecode interpretation, and a runtime model closer to PyPy than to mainline Python. **Pyston-lite** (2022) repackaged the JIT as a CPython extension (`pip install pyston-lite-autoload`) — same idea CinderX adopted three years later.

**Pyjion** (Microsoft, 2016–2021) is the third historical production JIT effort, using the .NET CLR's RyuJIT as the codegen backend for CPython bytecode. It demonstrated that a host JIT can be reused for a guest language but shipped as research and was archived in 2022.

sections.title: Design lessons
sections.content:
Both projects illustrate the difficulty of sustained engineering investment for an alternative Python implementation. Pyston was Dropbox's project until 2017, then went dormant when Dropbox refocused; revived in 2020, it eventually got eclipsed by the mainline CPython JIT and Cinder. Pyjion was a Microsoft research project; archived when its champion left.

The deployment lesson Pyston-lite established — JIT as CPython extension rather than fork — was important enough that CinderX adopted it. The technique angle still matters: copy-and-patch baselines, adaptive interpreters, and method-level JITs occupy distinct points in the same design space. The project-specific code is mostly historical interest now, but the design points remain relevant for new Python-acceleration efforts.
